description: Percent of applicants admitted: admits ÷ applicants. Lower is more selective. Note this is the institutional rate, not yield (enrollees ÷ admits). Source: IPEDS Admissions component (ADM).

ACT composite score at the 50th percentile of admitted students (midpoint between 25th and 75th percentiles, with a 25/75 average used as fallback when only band scores are reported). Kept separate from SAT — no concordance is applied. Useful for descriptive comparisons but the test-optional era means coverage varies widely.

Total number of applications received for the most recent fall cohort. A raw scale measure; very different at a 1,500-student liberal arts college vs. a 50,000-student state flagship. Use alongside acceptance_rate to interpret either. Source: IPEDS ADM.

Early decision acceptance rate: ED admits ÷ ED applicants. Lower means an ED program that's harder to crack. Reported in the Common Data Set, so coverage is roughly 45% of institutions (CDS respondents only) — NA values can mean either no ED program or just no submission.

Early decision applicants as a percent of total applicants. A signal of institutional reliance on ED: high values often correlate with selective privates that lock in their class before the regular decision cycle. CDS numerator (~45% coverage) over a near-universal IPEDS denominator.

Percent of first-time students who submitted an ACT score with their application. Important context for act_mid50 — small submission shares can produce unrepresentative midpoints, especially under test-optional policies. Source: IPEDS ADM (ACTPCT).

Percent of first-time students who submitted an SAT score with their application. Important context for sat_mid50; low submission shares under test-optional admissions can skew the midpoint toward the highest scorers. Source: IPEDS ADM (SATPCT).

Percent of enrolled first-year students who graduated in the top 10% of their high school class. A standard signal of admissions selectivity, but only the ~45% of institutions that respond to the Common Data Set report it.

Composite SAT (Verbal + Math) at the 50th percentile of admitted students, computed as the midpoint between 25th and 75th percentiles when needed. Test-optional policies mean this reflects the subset of admits who chose to submit scores — generally a higher-scoring slice — so trends over recent years are not directly comparable. Source: IPEDS ADM.

Yield rate gap (women minus men), in percentage points. Positive values mean women yielded at a higher rate than men. Exploratory variable — useful for spotting gender disparities in matriculation patterns, not used in peer-distance calculation.

Percent of admitted students who enrolled: enrollees ÷ admits. A signal of institutional draw — high yield means students chose this school over their alternatives. Independent of selectivity (high yield can coexist with either high or low acceptance rate). Source: IPEDS ADM.

Average federal loan amount per borrower (first-time, full-time undergraduates). Higher values usually indicate higher costs and/or higher unmet need at this institution. Source: IPEDS Student Financial Aid (SFA).

Average institutional grant or scholarship per recipient (first-time, full-time undergraduates). Combines need- and merit-based aid that comes from the institution itself; excludes federal Pell or state grants. Source: IPEDS SFA.

Average net price (cost of attendance minus all grant aid) across first-time, full-time undergraduates who received any aid. The single best indicator of what an aided family actually pays. Public institutions report in-state Title IV recipients; private nonprofits report all grant recipients. Source: IPEDS SFA.

Average net price for first-time, full-time, federal-aid-receiving undergraduates from families with adjusted income of $0–30,000. The most affordability-sensitive net-price slice; useful for evaluating access for low-income students. Public institutions report in-state band 1; private nonprofits report all. Source: IPEDS SFA.

Institutional grant aid as a percent of published tuition and fees. A direct measure of tuition discounting — how much sticker price the institution gives back as scholarships. High values (40%+) are common at private LACs; low values typify public flagships. Computed from IPEDS SFA + IC.

Percent of first-time, full-time undergraduates receiving any grant or scholarship aid (institutional, federal, state, or other). A breadth measure rather than a depth one — pairs well with avg_inst_grant for the full picture. Source: IPEDS SFA.
Percent of first-time, full-time undergraduates who took out a federal student loan. Higher values often reflect either higher cost or larger shares of students with unmet need. Source: IPEDS SFA.

Percent of freshmen whose full demonstrated financial need was met by the institution's aid package, from the Common Data Set. A more demanding bar than pct_need_met — captures whether the school covers gaps completely vs. partially. Survey respondents only (~45%).

Average percent of demonstrated financial need met across freshmen, from the Common Data Set. 100% means the school covers the gap between cost of attendance and expected family contribution; lower values mean families face unmet need. Survey respondents only (~45%).

Percent of first-time, full-time undergraduates receiving Pell grants. The standard proxy for an institution's economic-access profile. Pell eligibility roughly tracks family income under ~$60,000. Source: IPEDS SFA.

Total number of all undergraduates (not just first-time full-time) awarded Pell grants in the most recent year. A scale measure of how many low-income students an institution serves in absolute terms, complementing the percentage-based pct_pell. Source: IPEDS SFA.

Number of female student-athletes, unduplicated headcount — multi-sport athletes are counted once. From the federal EADA (Equity in Athletics Disclosure Act) filing. Use alongside total_athletes_undup for the school's full athletics scale.
Number of male student-athletes, unduplicated headcount — multi-sport athletes are counted once. Source: EADA institution-level filing.

Count of distinct men's varsity sports the institution sponsored in the most recent EADA filing. Wider sponsorship typically signals a larger athletics program. Single-sex institutions report zero on the opposite side. EADA 2024-25 covers ~2,037 schools.

Total athlete-sport participations divided by unduplicated athletes — i.e., the average number of varsity sports per athlete. Ratios above 1.20 suggest meaningful cross-sport participation, typical at smaller LACs where students play multiple sports. Computed from EADA.

Total unduplicated athletes as a percent of full-time undergraduates. Ranges from ~1% at large D-I publics to 40%+ at small D-III liberal arts colleges. The headline measure of how athletics-intensive a campus is. Computed from EADA.

Female athletes as a percent of female full-time undergraduate enrollment. EADA's gender-specific UG denominator. Useful for comparing women's athletics participation across very differently-sized institutions.
Male athletes as a percent of male full-time undergraduate enrollment. EADA's gender-specific UG denominator. Pair with pct_female_athletes to look at gender balance in athletics participation.
Total unduplicated student-athlete headcount. Sum of male and female athletes; the appropriate numerator whenever you compute athletes-per-enrollment. Source: EADA.

Total number of varsity sports sponsored (men's + women's). A pure breadth measure of program scale — a D-III school running 24 sports may sponsor twice as many programs as a D-I school running 12 well-resourced ones. Source: EADA.
Count of distinct women's varsity sports the institution sponsored in the most recent EADA filing. Title IX context makes this an important balance check against mens_varsity_sports. Source: EADA.

First-time degree-seeking undergraduate enrollment in fall — the entering cohort. Excludes returning students, transfers, and continuing students. The denominator for most retention and graduation rate calculations. Source: IPEDS EF (DRVEF.EFUG1ST).
Total full-time enrollment in fall, across undergraduate and graduate levels. The fastest way to gauge campus density during the academic year. Source: IPEDS EF (DRVEF.ENRFT).

Median family income of dependent students filing the FAFSA, as reported by College Scorecard. A direct measure of the typical student family's economic position. Note this only reflects FAFSA filers — students from higher-income families who didn't file are excluded, which can bias the median downward at wealthier institutions. Single 2024 snapshot.

Percent of undergraduates aged 25–64. High values signal commuter, adult-learner, or post-traditional populations; low values typify residential traditional-age colleges. Source: IPEDS EF age distribution.

Percent of total enrollment that is American Indian or Alaska Native (not Hispanic). Detailed-breakdown variable; for clustering, use the broader BIPOC / White / International / Unknown 3-level trio instead. Source: IPEDS EF.
Percent of total enrollment that is Asian (not Hispanic). Detailed-breakdown variable; the clustering version is the BIPOC aggregate. Source: IPEDS EF.

Percent of total enrollment identifying as a race/ethnicity other than non-Hispanic white (excluding international students and race-unknowns). The 3-level race/ethnicity trio used in clustering: BIPOC / White / International. Race-unknown students are intentionally excluded from this computation to keep the trio summing to 100. Source: IPEDS EF, derived.
Percent of total enrollment that is Black or African American (not Hispanic). Detailed-breakdown variable; the clustering version uses the BIPOC aggregate. Source: IPEDS EF.

Percent of undergraduates who are first-generation college students (neither parent holds a bachelor's degree), as reported by College Scorecard. A single 2024 snapshot computed from FAFSA filers — about 58% of undergraduates project FAFSAs, so this excludes students whose families didn't file.

Graduate share of total enrollment: 100% minus pct_undergrad. Tagged descriptive (not clustering) to avoid double-counting with pct_undergrad, since the two are algebraic complements. Useful for distinguishing primarily-undergraduate institutions from research universities. Source: IPEDS EF, derived.
Percent of total enrollment that is Hispanic or Latino (of any race). Detailed-breakdown variable; the clustering version uses the BIPOC aggregate. Source: IPEDS EF.
Percent of total enrollment that is U.S. nonresident (international students). Visa status, not race or ethnicity. Part of the 3-level race/ethnicity trio used in clustering. Source: IPEDS EF.
Percent of total enrollment that is Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander (not Hispanic). Detailed-breakdown variable; the clustering version uses the BIPOC aggregate. Source: IPEDS EF.

Percent of total enrollment that is part-time. The complement of full-time share. High values often signal commuter, working-adult, or community-college-adjacent populations. Source: IPEDS EF, derived.

Percent of total enrollment whose race/ethnicity was not reported to IPEDS. Excluded from the BIPOC computation. Mostly a data-completeness check — high values may indicate inconsistent reporting practices. Source: IPEDS EF.
Percent of total enrollment that identifies as two or more races (not Hispanic). Detailed-breakdown variable; the clustering version uses the BIPOC aggregate. Source: IPEDS EF.
Undergraduate share of total enrollment. High values (90%+) typify undergraduate-focused colleges; lower values reflect significant graduate or professional enrollment. Source: IPEDS EF, derived.
Percent of total enrollment that is non-Hispanic white. Part of the 3-level race/ethnicity trio used in clustering. Source: IPEDS EF.

Percent of undergraduates living in institution-owned, -operated, or -affiliated housing. The cleanest signal of residential vs. commuter character. Reported in the Common Data Set, so coverage is roughly 45% of institutions.

Total headcount of all students enrolled in the fall term — full-time and part-time, undergraduate and graduate. The single most common measure of an institution's overall size. Source: IPEDS EF (DRVEF.ENRTOT).

Transfer-in undergraduate enrollment in fall — students who began their undergraduate work elsewhere and entered this institution. A scale measure of transfer activity. Pair with first_time_enrollment to understand the entering cohort mix. Source: IPEDS EF.

Headcount of undergraduate students in the fall term — full-time + part-time. The most common measure of an institution's undergraduate scale. Excludes graduate and professional students. Source: IPEDS EF (DRVEF.EFUG).

Total core operating expenses divided by full-time-equivalent enrollment. Core expenses include instruction, research, public service, academic support, student services, institutional support, O&M, and depreciation. Excludes auxiliary services. Source: IPEDS Finance (F).

Endowment value expressed as years of core operating expenses (endowment ÷ annual core expenses). A standard financial-strength measure: most LACs run 1–5 years of coverage; the wealthiest research universities can run 10+. Source: IPEDS F, derived.
End-of-year endowment market value divided by full-time-equivalent enrollment. The standard per-student-resource measure used in higher-ed financial benchmarking. Source: IPEDS F.

Three-year average of total R&D expenditures from the NSF Higher Education Research and Development (HERD) survey, as reported in the Carnegie 2025 Public Data File. Headline indicator of research intensity. Excludes federally funded R&D centers. One-time snapshot covering FY2020–2023.

Total net assets at fiscal year-end, divided by FTE enrollment. Captures cumulative financial position (assets minus liabilities) per student. FASB and GASB reporters use different line items but they're combined here. Source: IPEDS F.

Operating margin per FTE, excluding investment return. Operating revenue minus core expenses, normalized by enrollment. Negative values are common at endowment-heavy schools that fund operations from investment income — that's the point of excluding investment return here. Source: IPEDS F, derived.

Published in-state undergraduate tuition plus required fees for the academic year. Sticker price, before any aid is considered. Use net price variables for what students actually pay. Source: IPEDS IC (TUITION2 + FEE2).

Net tuition revenue as a percent of core operating expenses. A stable analog to tuition dependence — high values (>80%) typify tuition-dependent schools; low values reflect heavy endowment or appropriations support. Uses expenses (not revenue) to avoid investment-return volatility. Source: IPEDS F, derived.

Number of research/scholarship doctorates awarded in the most recent year. Excludes professional-practice doctorates (MD, JD, etc.) — those are tracked separately. A NECHE peer-set member. Source: IPEDS Completions (C).

Earnings of an institution's graduates relative to predicted earnings, given demographic composition and CCIHE's SAEC (Social and Economic Mobility Classification) computation. Values above 1.0 mean graduates out-earn predictions; below 1.0 mean they under-earn. One-time Carnegie 2025 snapshot.

Six-year graduation rate for first-generation students specifically, reported via the Common Data Set. The rate itself (not the gap vs. non-first-gen) — construct gap comparisons in the inspector. Survey respondents only (~45%).

Four-year graduation rate (100% of normal time) for the most recent IPEDS first-time, full-time degree-seeking bachelor's cohort. Stricter bar than the 6-year rate; reflects on-time completion. Source: IPEDS Graduation Rates (GR).
Six-year graduation rate (150% of normal time) for the most recent IPEDS first-time, full-time degree-seeking bachelor's cohort. The standard benchmark for undergraduate completion. Source: IPEDS GR.
Six-year graduation rate gap, women minus men, in percentage points. Positive values mean women graduate at higher rates than men. Exploratory variable, not used in peer-distance computation.

Three-year loan repayment rate among federal borrowers who completed: the fraction of completer-borrowers whose loan balances are declining three years after entering repayment. Higher = better outcomes. From the latest available College Scorecard cohort.

Median annual earnings of federal-aid recipients ten years after entering the institution, from College Scorecard. A long-horizon outcomes signal — but reflects the cohort that entered roughly a decade ago, so it lags current programs. Latest available Scorecard cohort.
Median annual earnings of federal-aid recipients six years after entering the institution, from College Scorecard. Shorter-horizon companion to the 10-year measure. Latest available Scorecard cohort.

Number of distinct 2-digit CIP (Classification of Instructional Programs) families in which the institution awarded at least one graduate degree. Broader grouping than n_grad_programs — a CIP2 family covers a discipline area (e.g., Engineering) rather than a specific program. Source: IPEDS C, derived.

Number of distinct 6-digit CIP graduate programs in which the institution awarded at least one degree at the master's, research-doctorate, or professional-practice level. A breadth-of-graduate-offerings measure. Source: IPEDS C, derived.

Number of distinct 2-digit CIP undergraduate families in which the institution awarded at least one bachelor's degree. Broader grouping than n_undergrad_programs; each CIP2 represents a major academic area. Source: IPEDS C, derived.

Number of distinct 6-digit CIP undergraduate programs in which the institution awarded at least one bachelor's degree in the most recent year. A coarse breadth-of-curriculum measure. Counts only programs that actually produced graduates. Source: IPEDS C, derived.

Pell-grant recipients' 6-year award rate minus non-Pell recipients' 6-year award rate, in percentage points. Negative values mean Pell students fared worse than non-Pell peers at the institution. Source: IPEDS Outcome Measures (OM).

Full-time, first-year, first-time degree-seeking retention rate — the percent of an entering cohort that returned the following fall. Strong early signal of student engagement and academic fit, available years before any graduation rate. Source: IPEDS EF (RET_PCF).

Percent of an entering cohort that transferred to another institution rather than completing at the original. Some institutions (community colleges, regional publics) have transfer as an explicit mission; for residential bachelor's institutions a high transfer-out rate often signals fit issues. Source: IPEDS GR.

Academic support expenses (libraries, academic computing, academic administration, etc.) divided by FTE enrollment. A measure of investment in the academic infrastructure surrounding direct instruction. Source: IPEDS F.

Average salary of full-time instructional faculty on 9-month contracts (equated where needed for 12-month contracts). Reflects compensation levels and, by proxy, hiring competitiveness. Source: IPEDS Human Resources (SAL).
Instruction expenses (faculty salaries, departmental support, instructional supplies) per FTE enrollment. The most direct per-student measure of spending on actual teaching. Source: IPEDS F.

Instruction as a percent of total core operating expenses. Tracks how much of operating spend goes to direct teaching costs (faculty, departments) vs. research, public service, support, etc. Most LACs run 35–50%; research universities are lower because more spending flows to research. Source: IPEDS F.

Percent of undergraduate class sections with 50 or more students enrolled. Together with pct_classes_under_20, gives a coarse picture of class-size distribution. Common Data Set source — survey respondents only (~45%). Some institutions exclude labs/recitations differently.

Percent of undergraduate class sections with fewer than 20 students enrolled. A staple of college rankings — high values usually indicate small-seminar-rich curricula. Common Data Set source (~45% coverage). Both this and pct_classes_50plus depend on how the institution counts sections.

Number of full-time-equivalent students divided by full-time-equivalent instructional faculty. Lower numbers usually signal smaller classes. IPEDS doesn't publish STUFACR directly so this is computed from FTE enrollment and full-time-equivalent instructional staff. Matches the NSC and US News published versions for most institutions.

Student services expenses (admissions, registrar, financial aid, career services, student health, student activities, etc.) per FTE enrollment. Captures non-academic-but-essential investment per student. Source: IPEDS F.

Percent of full-time instructional staff who are tenured or on the tenure track (FACSTAT 20 + 30 over FACSTAT 0 total). Higher values usually signal more stable, research-active faculty cores. NA for institutions without a tenure system at all (typically community colleges and some specialty schools). Source: IPEDS HR.

Ordinal numeric encoding of NCAA / NAIA athletics division. Higher values represent more athletically intense programs: NCAA Division I = 3 (full scholarships, large programs, Holy Cross's tier), NCAA Division II and NAIA = 2 (partial scholarships or alternative national governance, similar competition intensity), NCAA Division III = 1 (no athletic scholarships, more academically focused). Schools coded 'Other' (alternative governance bodies or unaffiliated programs) are NA — we don't conflate them with a specific intensity tier. Schools with no varsity athletics also coded NA. The variable is synthetic: computed inside compute_peers from the categorical athletics_division field rather than read from a facts CSV. Useful in the Athletics theme alongside pct_athletes_overall (intensity), total_varsity_sports (breadth), and multi_sport_ratio (culture).
